data.title: Xiaomi lifts 2025 EV goal to 400,000 following break-even milestone
data.description:
• Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF) now expects to deliver more than 400,000 cars this year, showing growing momentum from an EV business that became profitable much faster than its competitors.
  • The electronics producer said in its third-quarter earnings call that it’s due to hit its earlier target of delivering 350,000 units by this week, according to a report by Bloomberg News.
  • Xiaomi's (OTCPK:XIACF) electric vehicle division made a profit for the first time in Q3, the EV arm posted an operating profit of 700 million yuan in the September quarter, after previously running losses.
  • Still, the company’s Hong Kong stock declined 3.7% on Thursday.

data.title: PulteGroup raises dividend by 18.2% to $0.26 a share
data.description:

data.title: Asia stocks trade mixed after Wall Street's post-Nvidia jump, while China plans property relief and holds rates
data.description:
Asia stocks trade mixed on Thursday following a Wall Street's rally after Nvidia’s strong earnings eased AI valuation concerns. Sentiment was also supported by the PBoC’s decision to keep key lending rates at record lows for a sixth straight meeting.

Nvidia surged more than 5% in extended trading after reporting stronger earnings and revenue along with a better-than-expected fourth quarter sales forecast, with CEO Jensen Huang saying demand for its Blackwell chips is “off the charts.” 

Gold prices fell below $4,070 per ounce on Thursday, following a two-day gain.

Japan (NKY:IND) rose 2.68% to around 50300 while the Topix gained 2.6% to 3330 on Thursday, with Japanese shares snapping a four-day decline. The Japanese yen weakened toward 157.5 per dollar on Thursday, languishing at ten month lows as investors braced for the announcement of Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s stimulus package, which is expected to exceed 20 trillion yen.

The Bank of Japan should continue normalizing interest rates to bring real rates back to equilibrium and avoid future distortions, board member Junko Koeda said in a speech Thursday. 

China (SHCOMP) fell 0.17% to above 3,950 while the Shenzhen Component lost 0.15% to 13,060 on Thursday, with mainland stocks showing mixed performances after China’s central bank kept key lending rates unchanged as expected, signalling no immediate policy easing, and the offshore yuan steadied around 7.11 per dollar on Thursday.

The one-year Loan Prime Rate ((LPR)), which serves as the benchmark for most corporate and household loans, was maintained at 3.0%. Similarly, the five-year LPR, the primary reference rate for mortgages, remained steady at 3.5%, following its last adjustment (a 10 basis point cut) in May 2025.

Chinese policymakers are reportedly considering a new, aggressive package of measures aimed at reviving their troubled housing market, as fears mount that the long crisis could now threaten the entire financial system, Bloomberg reported, citing sources familiar with the discussions. Bloomberg said the plan has been under discussion since Q3, though “timing and final policy design remain uncertain.”

China's rare earth magnet exports to the US surged 56.1% from a month earlier to 656 tons in October 2025, the highest since January, according to General Administration of Customs data released on Thursday. Year-to-date, China’s rare earth exports fell 5.2% compared with the same period in 2024, totaling 45,290 tons.

Financial and property stocks led gains, buoyed by strong demand for China’s EUR 4 billion euro-denominated bond sale, signaling robust investor confidence.

Meantime, Beijing reportedly plans to suspend imports of Japanese seafood, escalating tensions after Japanese PM Sanae Takaichi suggested Tokyo could respond militarily if China attacks Taiwan.

Investors now turn their attention to next week’s PMI releases for additional insight into the country’s economic health. 

Meanwhile, technology stocks advanced as Nvidia’s strong earnings report helped ease concerns over a potential artificial intelligence bubble.

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 0.41% to 25,907 on Thursday morning, snapping a four-day losing streak ahead of Hong Kong’s October inflation data due later today, following months of steady readings.

India (SENSEX) rose 0.28% to 85,323 in morning trade on Thursday, extending gains from the previous session. 

Australia (AS51) rose 1.24% to above 8,540 in early Thursday trade, moving away from over five-month lows. The Australian dollar traded around $0.647, hovering near its lowest level in six weeks, as investors assessed the Reserve Bank’s ongoing debate over how restrictive policy really is.

In the U.S. on Wednesday, all three major indexes ended higher, reversing some of the sharp selling from the prior four sessions as markets digested a divisive Fed minutes release and awaited Nvidia’s earnings after the close.

However, traders were cautious ahead of US jobs data, seeking clues on the Fed’s next policy move after FOMC minutes showed several officials were divided on a December interest rate cut.

U.S. stock futures climbed on Thursday as Nvidia’s quarterly beat and upbeat outlook reignited confidence in the artificial intelligence trade: Dow +0.60%; S&P 500 +1.23%; Nasdaq +1.74%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

data.title: Novartis projects 5–6% sales growth through 2030 as peak sales outlook for Kisqali, Scemblix increases
data.description:
Novartis (NVS) on Thursday rolled forward its mid-term guidance to 2025-2030, projecting 5-6% constant currency sales growth, driven by higher peak sales expectations for key brands.

The Swiss drugmaker raised its peak revenue expectations for Kisqali from $8 billion to $10 billion, and Scemblix from $3 billion to $4 billion.

The company now has eight de-risked, in-market assets with $3-10 billion peak sales potential, according to a statement, ahead of its meet Novartis management event in London today.

The company's pipeline includes 30+ potential high-value medicines, with 10+ licensed or acquired in the past two years.

Novartis delivered a core operating income margin of 41.2% in the first nine months of 2025 – two years ahead of plan – and expects to return to 40%+ margins by 2029, after absorbing 1-2 percentage points of dilution from the planned acquisition of Avidity Biosciences.

data.title: JOYY beats top-line and bottom-line estimates; gives FY outlook
data.description:
• JOYY press release (JOYY): Q3 Non-GAAP EPADS of $1.36 beats by $0.38.
  • Revenue of $540.2M (-3.3% Y/Y) beats by $7.85M.
  • Live streaming revenues were US$388.5 million, compared with US$439.5 million in the corresponding period of 2024, representing an increase of 3.5% from US$375.4 million in the second quarter of 2025.
  • Advertising revenues increased by 29.2% to US$112.5 million from US$87.1 million in the corresponding period of 2024 and by 17.1% from US$96.1 million in the second quarter of 2025.
  • Other revenues increased by 22.3% to US$39.2 million from US$32.1 million in the corresponding period of 2024 and by 8.3% from US$36.2 million in the second quarter of 2025.
  • Non-GAAP EBITDA was US$50.6 million, representing an increase of 16.8% from US$43.3 million in the corresponding period of 2024 and an increase of 4.9% from US$48.2 million
  • Net Cash as of September 30, 2025 was US$3,320.9 million.
  • Net cash from operating activities was US$73.4 million, compared with US$61.1 million in the corresponding period of 2024.
  • Global average mobile MAUs7 reached 266.2 million in the third quarter of 2025, compared with 272.4 million in the corresponding period of 2024. The global average mobile MAUs was up by 1.4% from 262.5 million in the second quarter of 2025.
  • Business Outlook: For the fourth quarter of 2025, the Company expects net revenues to be between US$563 million and US$578 million vs. consensus of $547.49M.

data.title: China considers aggressive new stimulus to rescue property sector amid financial system fears - report
data.description:
Chinese policymakers are reportedly considering a new, aggressive package of measures aimed at reviving their troubled housing market, as fears mount that the long crisis could now threaten the entire financial system, Bloomberg reported, citing sources familiar with the discussions.

As per the report, the urgency for new intervention is underscored by the deepening stress on the banking sector. The property crisis, combined with the weakened ability of households to service their debt, has led to warnings about deteriorating bank asset quality.

Officials, including those within the Housing Ministry, are exploring a range of options, including providing mortgage subsidies to new homebuyers nationwide (a first for national policy), raising income tax rebates for existing mortgage borrowers, and reducing the costs associated with home transactions, one of the people said.

The planned policies have been under discussion since at least the third quarter; however, the timing and specific details of implementation remain uncertain.

China's housing market remains trapped in a deep, four-year downturn that continues to weigh heavily on the national economy. The crisis has had a widespread negative impact, directly depressing domestic consumption, employment, and overall household wealth.

While earlier government support briefly stabilized the sector, that momentum quickly faded, with recent data showing home sales declining since the second quarter and fixed-asset investment collapsing last month.

Fitch Ratings analysts issued a warning last month that Chinese banks’ asset quality could deteriorate further next year. This concern follows a surge in non-performing loans, which reached a record high of 3.5 trillion yuan (US$492 billion) by the end of September.

The worsening outlook for the property market, combined with a diminished capacity for households to repay mortgages and personal loans, poses a serious risk to the financial sector.

In an effort to stabilize the sector and mitigate this systemic risk, the government's plan to subsidise new mortgages is intended to lure reluctant homebuyers back into the free-falling market and stabilize prices.

On Thursday, the Shanghai Composite rose 0.2% to above 3,950 while the Shenzhen Component lost 0.15% to 13,060 on Thursday, with mainland stocks showing mixed performances after China’s central bank kept key lending rates unchanged as expected, and the offshore yuan steadied around 7.11 per dollar on Thursday.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NYSEARCA:KWEB), (NYSEARCA:CQQQ), (NASDAQ:MCHI), (NYSEARCA:ASHR), (NYSEARCA:YINN), (NYSE:TDF), (NYSEARCA:CHIQ), (NYSEARCA:GXC), (NYSEARCA:EWH), (NYSEARCA:KBA), (NYSEARCA:YANG), (NASDAQ:CXSE), (NYSE:CAF), (NYSEARCA:CWEB), (NASDAQ:PGJ), (NYSEARCA:KURE).

Currency: (CNY:USD)

data.title: Meta's chief AI scientist Yann LeCun announces departure to start his own firm
data.description:
Meta's (META) chief AI scientist and the founding director of the FAIR lab, Yann LeCun, is leaving the company at the end of 2025, according to an official memo posted on his LinkedIn account late on Wednesday. 

LeCun, who has been with the social media and tech giant for 12 years, said he plans to start a new company and will partner with Meta and give it access to its innovations. 

In his public post, LeCun said he is going to create a startup company to continue the "Advanced Machine Intelligence" research program he has been pursuing over the last several years with colleagues at FAIR, at New York University, and other places.

"The goal of the startup is to bring about the next big revolution in AI: systems that understand the physical world, have persistent memory, can reason, and can plan complex action sequences," LeCun said.

The announcement of LeCun's departure also comes amid a major C-suite rejig at Meta, which saw the exit of revenue chief John Hegeman and Clara Shih, the head of Business AI.

LeCun's job at the Mark Zuckerberg-led company focused on long-term AI research, which won’t play a part in shaping consumer experiences in the near future. According to sources who spoke to Bloomberg, LeCun had difficulty getting resources for his projects as Meta focused more intently on building models to compete with immediate threats from rivals including OpenAI (OPENAI), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Anthropic PBC (ANTHRO).

After concluding Meta had fallen behind key rivals, Zuckerberg shifted his focus away from the research efforts of FAIR and toward faster deployment of AI models and products. Over the summer, he brought in Alexandr Wang to run a new "superintelligence" division, spending $14.3B to hire the 28-year-old Scale AI founder and acquire a 49% stake in his company.

In October, the company laid off 600 employees in its AI division but retained top talent to increase efficiency and offset the impact of its aggressive capital spending on building critical AI infrastructure.
